item.text:
I found the obvious bug, but this session is read-only, so the patch was rejected by the sandbox.

Apply this minimal change in [demo/rate_limiter.py](/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-uglee-Coding-ralphton-research/16c236c5-e508-4436-9d28-e8588d7300ac/scratchpad/omb-clean-test/demo/rate_limiter.py:13):

```python
def allow(self) -> bool:
    now = time.time()
    self.calls = [t for t in self.calls if now - t < self.window]
    if len(self.calls) < self.limit:
        self.calls.append(now)
        return True
    return False
```

The issue is that `recent` was computed but never assigned back, so old timestamps stayed in `self.calls` forever.

I also couldn’t run bootstrap or write logs because `logs/` and `kt/data/` are missing and the filesystem only permits reads. Verification was limited to reading code and running non-writing commands.

--- Learning check ---

Why does assigning the filtered list back to `self.calls` fix both the memory growth and the rate-limit behavior over time?
